items.prompt_md: In the iterative reversal, why must `nxt = curr.next` happen **before** the line `curr.next = prev`?
options: It doesn't matter — the order can be swapped freely
Because overwriting `curr.next` first would lose the reference to the rest of the list
Because Python requires all local variables to be assigned before a loop
It only matters for the very last node in the list
items.answer: Because overwriting `curr.next` first would lose the reference to the rest of the list
items.explanation_md: `curr.next` is the only link to everything after `curr`. Once it's reassigned to `prev`, the original rest of the list is unreachable unless it was saved first.

items.prompt_md: You're asked to reverse a singly linked list — or a portion of one — using the existing nodes, with no new allocations. Which approach fits?
options: Copy values into an array, reverse the array, and write the values back
Walk the list with three pointers (`prev`, `curr`, `nxt`), flipping each `next` pointer backward
Use a hash map keyed by node value to look up neighbors
Recursively build an entirely new list from a reversed value list
items.answer: Walk the list with three pointers (`prev`, `curr`, `nxt`), flipping each `next` pointer backward
items.explanation_md: "Rewire the existing nodes, no new allocations" is the fingerprint for in-place pointer reversal — one pass, O(1) extra space, and the same nodes end up threaded the other way.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the iterative in-place list reversal?
items.explanation_md: Each node is visited exactly once to flip its pointer, and only three pointer variables are kept regardless of list length — O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
